summary: American architect, designer, and futurist (1895–1983). Pioneered the geodesic dome; designed the Montreal Biosphere for Expo 67. Created the Dymaxion House (aluminum, circular, O-volving shelf, fog gun, descended from '4D Lightful Tower'), Dymaxion Car (three-wheeled, co-designed with Isamu Noguchi), and Dymaxion Map (icosahedron projection, profiled in Life magazine). Coined 'Spaceship Earth,' 'ephemeralization,' and 'tensegrity.' Namesake of the carbon-60 buckyball molecule. Coxeter's geometry was a major inspiration for his work.

works.name: Geodesic Dome / Montreal Biosphere (Expo 67)
works.description: Fuller's most celebrated design: a spherical structure distributing stress over polyhedra made solely of triangular faces. His most famous example is the US Pavilion at the 1967 Montreal Expo (Expo 67), built on St. Helen's Island. After being damaged by fire, the steel skeleton became the environmental museum 'Biosphere.' The structure embodies his principle of 'doing more with less' via interlocking triangles. His Geoscope at Expo 67 was also notable. The Eden Project in Cornwall, England (by Nicholas Grimshaw) was inspired by Fuller's geodesic domes. Epcot at Disney World (Spaceship Earth) emulates his style — made of two domes. H.S.M. Coxeter's work with geometry was a major inspiration for Fuller's designs. CHARAS, a New York City group, received a city loan to create public housing in his fashion.

works.name: Dymaxion House
works.description: A circular, factory-made aluminum house suspended from a central mast with 'bicycle-wheel' spokes and hexagonal walls (which made it hard to hang pictures). Descended from Fuller's earlier concept, the '4D Lightful Tower.' Prototypes 'Barwise' and 'Danbury' built with Wichita's Beech Aircraft. Features included an 'O-volving shelf' (revolving closet) and a 'fog gun' (or 'fogger') instead of a shower. Shape compared to a Hershey's Kiss. One surviving prototype purchased by the Henry Ford Museum in 2011 (in Dearborn, Michigan). The Wichita House was a slightly modified round version that used the 'dome effect' to regulate temperature. Later designs included the Growth Home of 1952 (which included a proto-vertical indoor farm). An early version of the bathroom was made of two connected copper bubbles with strategically-placed drains. Intended to feature translucent ceilings and downdraft ventilation. A Carbondale, Illinois example attracted children who tried to climb on its roof.

clues.clue: Circular aluminum house; prototypes 'Barwise' and 'Danbury' built with Beech Aircraft in Wichita

works.name: Dymaxion Map / Dymaxion Car / Dymaxion concept
works.description: Fuller coined 'Dymaxion' (portmanteau of dynamic, maximum, tension) as a prefix for many projects. The Dymaxion Map projects Earth's surface onto an icosahedron, which unfolds without directional distortion; profiled in Life magazine and precedes the Mercator-Robinson projections for area accuracy; Jasper Johns created a painting of 22 triangular canvas panels named for the 'airocean world' and the Dymaxion map. The Dymaxion Car was a three-wheeled vehicle (funded by Henry Kaiser), intended to 'hop off the road at will' as a 'zoomobile'; co-designed with Isamu Noguchi; Noguchi drove it with Clare Boothe Luce; documented as having fatal accidents (recorded by Alec Nevala-Lee). The Dymaxion Chronofile recorded Fuller's activities every 15 minutes for 63 years. The 'Dymaxion sleep' scheme consisted of half-hour naps once every six hours.

clues.clue: 'Dymaxion' portmanteau of 'dynamic,' 'maximum,' and 'tension': used for house, car, map, Chronofile, and sleep

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Richard Buckminster Fuller (1895–1983): American architect, designer, and systems thinker. Coined 'Spaceship Earth' (1968 book framed as its 'operating manual,' introducing concept of 'Great Pirates'). Coined 'tensegrity' (tensional integrity): structural design where compressed components are isolated within a network of pre-tensioned cables. Developed Synergetics: a design philosophy exploring intrinsic properties of shapes using 60-degree coordination. Defined 'ephemeralization' as doing everything with nothing through technological efficiency. Promoted 'design science.' Second World President of Mensa. Epitaph: 'Call me Trim Tab' — derived from a 1972 Playboy interview where he used the metaphor of a trim tab building low pressure to explain how individuals can change the world. Named the carbon-60 molecule 'buckminsterfullerene' (buckyball). Proposed Old Man River City (a giant dome over a crater for a new East St. Louis). Proposed floating spherical habitats 'Cloud Nine' held together by tensegrity (with Shoji Sadao). Professor at Southern Illinois University; close friends with choreographer Katherine Dunham who joined the same department. Collaborated with John McHale on the World Design Science Decade (1965). Collaborated with Shoji Sadao on the Dymaxion Map. Jasper Johns created a painting of twenty-two triangular canvas panels inspired by Fuller. Isamu Noguchi created a portrait bust (chrome-plated) of Fuller early in his career. Student J. Baldwin (Pillow Dome inventor) was chief designer of the Whole Earth Catalog. Louis Kahn referred to Fuller's space frames as 'hollow stones.' H.S.M. Coxeter's geometry was a major inspiration for Fuller's work. Also lived in a hemispherical structure designed to be easy to assemble quickly.

comprehensive_summary:
Richard Buckminster Fuller (1895–1983) was an American architect, designer, and systems thinker celebrated for pioneering the geodesic dome and coining the term 'Spaceship Earth.' His ethos of 'doing more with less' — which he called 'ephemeralization' — pervaded every aspect of his work, from architecture to cartography. H.S.M. Coxeter's work with geometry was a major inspiration for Fuller's designs.

Fuller's most iconic building is the geodesic dome: a spherical structure that distributes stress across polyhedra made solely of triangular faces. His most famous example is the US Pavilion at Expo 67, the 1967 Montreal World's Fair, built on St. Helen's Island. After being damaged by fire (nine years later, destroying a thin acrylic membrane covering the steel tubes), its skeleton became the environmental museum 'Biosphere.' Fuller also designed a Geoscope for the same event. His geodesic ideas were emulated at Epcot in Disney World (Spaceship Earth, made of two domes) and inspired the Eden Project in Cornwall by Nicholas Grimshaw. WWII-era circular steel shelters Fuller designed were inspired by the sight of metal grain silos (also described as 'deployment units' inspired by steel grain bins in Midwest agriculture). A New York City group called CHARAS received a city loan to create public housing in his geodesic dome fashion.

Fuller's domestic design work centered on the Dymaxion House: a circular aluminum structure descended from his earlier '4D Lightful Tower' concept, suspended from a central mast with 'bicycle-wheel' spokes and hexagonal walls (which made it hard to hang pictures). Prototypes named 'Barwise' and 'Danbury' were built in partnership with Wichita's Beech Aircraft. The house featured an 'O-volving shelf' (a revolving closet) and a 'fog gun' or 'fogger' instead of a conventional shower or bath. Its shape has been compared to a Hershey's Kiss. One prototype was purchased in 2011 by the Henry Ford Museum in Dearborn, Michigan — the only surviving example. The Wichita House was a slightly modified round variant that used the 'dome effect' to regulate temperature; the 1952 Growth Home included a proto-form of a vertical indoor farm. An early version of the bathroom was made of two connected copper bubbles with strategically-placed drains. The house was intended to feature translucent ceilings and downdraft ventilation. Fuller himself lived in and was associated with a hemispherical structure designed to be easy to assemble quickly. In Carbondale, Illinois, a Dymaxion house attracted children who tried to climb on the roof.

Fuller coined the portmanteau 'Dymaxion' (dynamic + maximum + tension) to brand his most forward-looking projects. The Dymaxion Map projects Earth's surface onto an icosahedron, which can be unfolded to two dimensions with minimal directional distortion, outperforming the Mercator and Robinson projections in area accuracy; it was profiled in Life magazine. Jasper Johns created a large painting made of 22 triangular canvas panels, named for the 'airocean world' and the Dymaxion map, inspired by Fuller. The Dymaxion Car was a three-wheeled 'zoomobile' co-designed with Isamu Noguchi; funded by Henry Kaiser; intended to 'hop off the road at will'; Noguchi drove it with Clare Boothe Luce; Alec Nevala-Lee documented its fatal accidents. The Dymaxion Chronofile recorded Fuller's activities every 15 minutes for 63 years. The 'Dymaxion sleep' scheme consisted of taking half-hour naps once every six hours.

Fuller coined 'tensegrity' (tensional integrity): a structural design approach in which compressed components are isolated from each other within a network of pre-tensioned cables. He developed Synergetics, a design philosophy that used 60-degree rather than 90-degree coordination to explore the intrinsic properties of shapes; his essay 'The Wellspring of Reality' decries specialism in favor of generalism. He proposed Cloud Nine: giant floating spherical tensegrity habitats (with Shoji Sadao), which would remain airborne by keeping internal temperature one degree higher than the surrounding atmosphere. He proposed Old Man River City, a massive new East St. Louis covered by a giant umbrella over a crater. Collaborating with John McHale, he inaugurated the World Design Science Decade in 1965 while a professor at Southern Illinois University, where he also became close friends with choreographer Katherine Dunham. His 1968 book was framed as an 'operating manual' for 'Spaceship Earth,' introducing the concept of 'Great Pirates.' Fuller was the second World President of Mensa. His epitaph reads 'Call me Trim Tab,' derived from a 1972 Playboy interview in which he used the metaphor of a trim tab building low pressure to illustrate individual empowerment. The carbon-60 molecule — buckminsterfullerene or the 'buckyball' — is named for him. Isamu Noguchi sculpted a chrome-plated portrait bust of Fuller early in his career; the two were lifelong friends who met at a tavern suggested by Constantin Brancusi. His student J. Baldwin, inventor of the Pillow Dome, was the chief designer of the Whole Earth Catalog. Louis Kahn described Fuller's space frames as 'hollow stones.'

Architect: Dymaxion House prototypes named 'Barwise' and 'Danbury' were built with Wichita's Beech Aircraft; the only surviving prototype was purchased in 2011 by the Henry Ford Museum
